name: Tlazolteotl
description: Tlazolteotl, also known as Tlaelquani ('Eater of Filth'), is a powerful and complex goddess in Aztec mythology. She is primarily associated with purification, confession, and healing, particularly concerning sexual transgressions. Tlazolteotl embodies the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, reflecting the Aztec worldview. She is the patroness of midwives, healers, and those seeking absolution from sins. Originating from the Huastec region, her worship spread throughout Mesoamerica, becoming a prominent aspect of Aztec religious life. Tlazolteotl's power lies in her ability to absorb and cleanse the 'filth' or sins of individuals through ritual confession. This process allowed individuals to be reborn, free from the burden of their past actions. She is often depicted with a blackened mouth, symbolizing her consumption of sins, and wearing a headdress adorned with spindles, representing weaving and the cycle of creation. While she is associated with sexual impurity, her role is ultimately one of purification and renewal, offering a path to spiritual cleansing within the Aztec belief system. Her complex nature extends to her association with fertility and childbirth, further emphasizing her connection to the cyclical processes of life and death. She is often seen as both a source of temptation and a source of redemption, highlighting the duality inherent in human nature.
subtitle: Goddess of Purification and Confession

keyMyths.title: The Eater of Filth
keyMyths.description: Tlazolteotl consumes the sins confessed to her, purifying the individual. This act allows for spiritual rebirth and a fresh start.

keyMyths.title: Patroness of Midwives
keyMyths.description: She is invoked by midwives to ensure safe and successful childbirths, reflecting her connection to fertility and new life.

keyMyths.title: Confession and Absolution
keyMyths.description: Aztecs confessed their sins to priests who acted as intermediaries with Tlazolteotl, seeking her forgiveness and cleansing.

worshipPractices.name: Confession Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Individuals would confess their sins, particularly sexual transgressions, to a priest who acted as an intermediary with Tlazolteotl. This confession was believed to cleanse the individual and prevent illness or misfortune.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of maize, copal incense, and other valuable items were made to Tlazolteotl to seek her favor and assistance. These offerings were often presented at her temples or during festivals dedicated to her.

festivals.name: Ochpaniztli
festivals.description: Ochpaniztli, or the 'sweeping' festival, was dedicated to various earth goddesses, including Tlazolteotl. It involved sweeping rituals, offerings of food and flowers, and performances to ensure a bountiful harvest and continued fertility.

cultural.modernLegacy: Though not actively worshipped, Tlazolteotl remains a figure of academic interest, representing Aztec views on sexuality, morality, and the cyclical nature of existence. She is sometimes invoked in modern neopagan and feminist contexts as a symbol of female power and sexual liberation.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The precise origins of Tlazolteotl are somewhat obscure, predating the rise of the Aztec empire. She is believed to have roots in earlier Mesoamerican cultures, possibly linked to similar earth and fertility goddesses. Her name, meaning 'Goddess of Filth' or 'Eater of Filth', reflects her association with absorbing and purifying impurities. She is often depicted wearing cotton garments and a headdress, sometimes with facial markings indicating her mature and powerful status. Some accounts describe her as an aspect of the earth goddess, Tonantzin, highlighting her connection to fertility and the life-giving properties of the earth. Her multifaceted nature suggests a complex evolution, incorporating various beliefs and practices over time. The Tepehua people, neighbors to the Aztecs, also revered Tlazolteotl, indicating her widespread influence in the region.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Tlazolteotl doesn't feature prominently in many lengthy narratives, her role in the confession ritual is central to her mythology. It was believed that she could absolve individuals of their sins, particularly those related to illicit sexual activity. However, this absolution came with a price: the individual had to fully confess their transgressions to a priest, who then acted as an intermediary. This act of confession was not merely a symbolic gesture; it was believed to directly impact the individual's health and well-being. Failing to confess could lead to illness, misfortune, or even death. Another myth associates her with the birth of Quetzalcoatl. In this version, she becomes pregnant after swallowing a jade, highlighting her connection to fertility and the creation of new life. She is also associated with the Tzitzimime, star demons who threatened to devour humanity during eclipses, further cementing her role as a powerful and complex figure.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Tlazolteotl's worship involved a complex system of rituals and beliefs. Confession was a central element, with priests playing a crucial role in facilitating communication between individuals and the goddess. These priests were highly trained and knowledgeable about the appropriate procedures for confession and absolution. Temples dedicated to Tlazolteotl were sites of pilgrimage, where individuals would seek her aid and offer sacrifices. The Ochpaniztli festival was a significant event in her honor, involving sweeping rituals, dances, and offerings to ensure fertility and a bountiful harvest. The attire of Tlazolteotl's priests and priestesses often reflected her association with purification, using white garments and symbolic objects. Her cult was particularly important for women, who sought her guidance and protection during childbirth and other life stages. The use of hallucinogenic substances was also possibly part of her cult, as it was with several other Aztec deities.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The influence of Tlazolteotl extended beyond the religious sphere, impacting Aztec social and moral codes. Her association with confession and purification reinforced the importance of honesty and moral behavior within the community. The fear of divine retribution for unconfessed sins served as a deterrent against illicit activities. While the Spanish conquest led to the suppression of traditional Aztec religious practices, the figure of Tlazolteotl continues to fascinate scholars and artists. Her complex and multifaceted nature, encompassing both the sacred and the profane, makes her a compelling subject of study. In modern times, Tlazolteotl has been reinterpreted by some as a symbol of female empowerment and sexual liberation, challenging traditional notions of morality and celebrating the cyclical nature of life and death. Her legacy serves as a reminder of the rich and complex worldview of the Aztec civilization.

shortDescription: Tlazolteotl is the Aztec goddess of purification, confession, and healing, particularly related to sexual transgressions. She embodies the cycle of life, death, and rebirth.
